speaker: interviewer
text: --the fate of your family? Can we now go back to the period of end of '39, beginning of 1940. What happened to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I mentioned before that the Russians took my husband because he was an engineer, and they needed him in the wartime. Since then, I haven't seen him. I was left alone with a very young-- it was actually a baby. And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] never heard anything more. But a Pole who lived on the same-- mostly Ukrainians, and some Poles, lived opposite. And he knew me and my husband-- a young man. He came back from-- when the Germans were-- the beginning. Actually the Germans were not-- that was the Russians. They were not in Lwow, they were in Przemysl, not far from Lwow. And otherwise he wouldn't come over, and he wouldn't know that I am alive there. He came over-- on the street, actually, I have seen him. And he said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have seen your husband. We were both [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Russia. When the Russians came forward [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Lwow, he was with him. And then they went over. They pushed out the Germans. That's why they came in. Later they came back, the Germans came back. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they caught-- I don't know-- a division or how many-- the Germans, not far from Tarnopol-- that was before Lwow-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about, I don't know, a whole division of Russians. The Germans took over-- took them with them. And they went in a transport. They put somewhere-- I don't know where, exactly-- but past Lwow, somewhere. And my husband wanted-- he has seen it-- he says. I don't know to believe him or not-- that he has seen that my husband wanted to jump out from a train in motion. He thought that no one will see him jump out. And he wanted to come back to us. But the Germans have seen him jumping out, and they shot him. That's what he told me. But my husband never came back. Don't know to believe him or not, but it's all the same. He was killed by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would this have been in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been-- no, end of '41. I think it was the end of '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we never heard of him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the Russians were in Lwow, right up to the end of the Russian occupation of Lwow, you were still working in the laboratory. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-- no. When the Russians fled, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no one else. I had to go back. That was-- But during the Russians-- that's another story-- who helped me a lot, too-- a Ukrainian-- non-Jew. During the Russians, when I worked, it was nearly the end of their occupation. Working at the laboratory there, the cashier-- the man who paid us our salaries was a Ukrainian. I don't remember his name. Or I don't know if I even knew his name. And he knew me. Don't know how well, but he knew me. I was then young and maybe not too bad-looking. So he always helped me out. And anything I needed, he was there. He helped me. And then, towards the end, the Russians were still there, and he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] left us, not telling anyone else. But we found out that he went over to the Germans. Ukrainians-- he was a sympathizer. Went over with all the money and didn't pay us anymore. That's why I was left without money. And when the Germans-- and the Russians, of course, fled. The Germans came over. And that must be at the end of '41, '41 at the end, because I was still living there. But later on-- I think I told this story when I went to the-- I was still in my own home, on [INAUDIBLE]. And I think I said who helped me there. I was hidden there many times. And that must have been in '42. Because in '42 I was-- I still didn't say about it-- imprisoned in Lackiego. The Prison was named Lackiego because it was on Lackiego Street, and it was a prison for criminals. There were two prisons in Lwow. One was on [INAUDIBLE]-- hard prisoners. Now Lackiego, there were political, too-- prisoners-- imprisoned there. And I don't want to go back to the story-- maybe it will come later-- about why I was imprisoned. But after the prison, when I-- and, again, a priest helped me. And that's a long story how he helped me. But when I was freed from prison, later on, of course I didn't go back, straight. And my daughter was left with the Polish family-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] small family-- a woman with two children, alone. Very poor, and very awful conditions. I just-- even now, if I think back of it, I don't know how she survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But when I came back-- not straightaway as I came back, because I was afraid someone was behind me to see where I'm going. They could find out that I'm not the one that I said in the prison I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a night I spent in the city from place to place, in the dark, walking around. About this I will tell later. It's another story. Finally, the next day, looking around if no one is behind me, I went to see my child, back there, to the place where my daughter was left as a young child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I came in-- and I had to go there late at night, because my daughter was left alone there. And that woman with her two children was-- I mentioned before, too, she was on a, a small marketplace, selling bread. I mentioned it before. So I had to wait until she will come home, about 9 or 10 o'clock at nighttime. And, at this time, I was afraid-- out of the prison, I was afraid to go in, that someone will see me. So I hid in toilets, there, from place-- from one toilet to another one. People wanted to go to the toilet, and I was there. So I got out. Didn't say-- I was afraid to say anything-- a word, even, to utter, because maybe someone will start asking questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went out and, as the person went out, I went back in. And so I waited till night, until I could hear the voices of that-- the children, that woman going in. And then quickly I went into her place. And I slept the night over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't tell before how my child was back from hospital-- how she was rescued, but that was much later. And I didn't say a word to her. I could only kiss her and didn't tell her anything. She didn't know, and I disappeared-- Because the Polish woman said to me, look, you survived the prison. Now the police will be after you, again, searching. And maybe if you go out, they'll go after you and find out that I keep your child here. And that was forbidden, of course, with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you'd better go out on the street and there, eh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, the Ukrainians are there-- they're rounding up people and sending them to Germany for work-- to Germany. Forced labor. So she said, you better go out. And so I did. I didn't say anything to my child, and she was crying, and I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew, if I stay there-- she said, the police will-- after you and find out. So, to rescue her, I thought I'd better go out. She will be alive there. And if they kill me, they'll kill me. What will happen-- I can't do anything about it. But they rounded up people. And, not knowing that I'm Jewish-- no one knew-- I told you about the birth certificate I had in my pocket. But they didn't ask for anything. And they didn't suspect any Jews to be on the streets there, because there were no Jews anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they rounded us up and took us all, a group of people, younger and older one, men and women, to a place. And I still remember where it was. And it was like, a-- like a huge camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there they herded all the people to send to forced labor, to Germany. It was on the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And, as I came in, there was a big fence around, and inside were not hundreds but thousands of people, inside. And there was one small building that was an office, there. We had all the people-- Again, my heart was beating all the time. I thought, now here they'll find me out, and here they will kill me. I've seen something else there that I'll tell later. And there was a huge queue, going into the-- before they sent us to Germany, they had to, one by one, to-- eh-- I think they had to enlist them all-- all the people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And because not everybody there was sent to Germany-- all to the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I waited. And I thought, no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll tell later how I got to the prison. It's always-- I have a method. I always thought-- I don't know-- I learned it during the war-- not to go the first, but the last-- always the last.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, see what will happen for the others. And I didn't want the others to see what happens to me, if they find out. So I will be the last. So I waited. Whenever a one came, I let them all before me, to go in-- one by one, went into the office. And I-- until I was the last. I went in. And I thought, now is my last-- not my hour, my last two minutes. They will kill me here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was surprised. I came in there. Behind the desk, who sits there? The man-- the Ukrainian who went over to the Germans, who worked with our-- who paid us the wages-- [INAUDIBLE], or whoever-- at the laboratorium where I worked, who knew me very well. And he was sitting behind the desk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in. And as I seen him, my knees were so-- got soft. And I thought, now I'll faint here. Don't faint, I told myself. Be strong. Don't faint. You'll see what happens. You wait. Rather, wait. And he said-- before I said-- he noticed it. And before I uttered a word, he said to me-- he knew my name-- my-- Ida Mehr. He knew my name. He said, in Ukrain-- I knew Ukrainian. I mentioned I speak Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what's your name now? 'Now.' I said, Wanda Grabowienska. Not a word-- not a smile. Just very serious. Before, the other-- these other door at the other side, we came in here and went out the other door [INAUDIBLE] when he said, wait a minute. Gave me a big bread. A big loaf, a big bread. Everybody before went out-- got, for the way-- because we had to travel for days-- everyone got a bread and some money-- not much, but some money he gave me that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is Ukrainian, 'Go with God.' It means, God will help me. He knew my God is a different god. So he knew that I believe that God will help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't say a word-- nothing. Just, what's your name now? And then he said-- he gave me bread, like all of them-- all of the people who went through there got a bread and some small change of money-- and said, go with God-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And so I went out the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: He rescued me. He could have killed me straightaway, or give me over-- the Gestapo was surrounding around everywhere. Then I, I, I was lucky. I don't know why I and not my parents, not my sisters-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the other people that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went out-- must tell you-- it's another story-- the other side. I went to the big yard where thousands and thousands of people. We had to wait there about two days, until the train. On the other side, there was the station-- a big station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had to wait there. I think that was for-- not for people. I think for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] horses or something like it. So there were-- we traveled not in normal trains. They were trains-- the Germans always use those trains, you know. And, as I waited on that-- we had to wait two days. These two days were the worst of all. Because I was rescued here, I was rescued before, and I thought, I don't think that-- no one can rescue you here. There were people who couldn't have known me from Lwow. There were people from not only the whole district-- from the whole part of Poland. Thousands of people, sent to forced labor. You know, there were-- I don't know-- more than 10,000. And these people walked-- everybody walked outside. The beds were on the other side. And they were not normal beds to sleep. We were there for two nights before the train came.

speaker: interviewer
text: We slept on the floor. There was not hay-- hard. I don't know what it is. You know? Without anything. Just on the floors, we slept all there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, as I walked-- it was very hard. Because-- not only difficult, but it was dangerous-- very dangerous, there. Because we walked around. I was alone. I had nothing but-- one thing, that I was dressed more like Ukrainians. When I was at the Polish woman, she gave me some clothes, and I just left mine-- and like a Ukrainian girl, something with a big skirt and a blouse. And I had nothing with me, only what that man gave me-- that bread-- and that was all. And, as I walked around, behind, there were always some Ukrainians-- these were the worst, except a few good people, you know. And they were behind me-- walking, always, behind me. I don't know-- I don't know why. Maybe because it's a Ukrainian girl, maybe a-- some men behind me, you know. And I-- I just went out of their way, all the time. If they talked to me, I didn't even answer. It was better not to answer than to answer. And once, as I stood there, suddenly I had a look-- just before my eyes-- a woman-- that what I want to tell you. A woman with a daughter-- a middle-aged woman, and a young daughter, I don't know, maybe 18, 20. They were both dressed in black, and hats-- very elegant. Some people, I found out later, came from Warsaw to Lwow-- travelled when-- not as Jews, in mourning. That's why they were all in black. They disguised themself, that they are in mourning. A woman-- very elegant. And had two cases. She carried the big case. I'll never forget that picture. She carried the big case, and her daughter carried a case. And because they were different than other people, they stood out of the crowd. As to-- go to-- How they got there, I don't know-- to-- sent to forced labor. But it was silly to dress like this. They thought they dress-- they disguise themself, and no one will suspect anything. But, because they stood out of the crowd-- I was dressed as a poor Ukrainian girl, not that-- old clothes, just not new clothes. But these were dressed very elegant, in mourning. And a Gestapo man came up to them and asked them to open the case. She refused. Asked the daughter, open the case. She refused. So, before shooting them, he shot into the case. It opened. Gold and silver. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was very stupid. They wanted to save not only their lives but even gold and silver. It was just, you know, a lot of chains and, and all kinds of silver and rings and everything. Full cases. One, and the other one. They were Jewish people, both.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first the case-- open, and then-- and just everybody looked on-- shot her and shot-- the mother and the daughter. And, of course, they were both Jewish. That's how-- there were many people like that, because they want to-- just to save their money, they paid with their lives. What happened to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And two days later, it was very, very difficult. You know, we slept together, men and women-- you know how it was. And, to stay-- to stay there, away from them, it was very difficult. But I did it. And then we went to Germany. They sent us. They came-- the, the train. Not-- as I said, not a normal train. Quickly-- it was quickly fabricated, something, like for, for animals. And we were sent to Germany. And that's a new story-- how I came to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would that have been, when you were deported to Germany? What year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- that was in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Summer or winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I don't know exactly. No--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was hot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must have been somewhere in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: About August.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how I came to Germany. But I didn't say how I got into prison-- Lackiego-- and how I got out from prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just go back--

speaker: interviewee
text: For that, I have to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was about August, September, '42, when you were deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been something like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I didn't know exactly what month, what time, what hour-- I had-- one didn't think of all of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were being processed for deportation, did all of the people pass through the process, or were some taken somewhere else-- or shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: About other people, I don't know, because I was there, only. And I've seen only what goes on-- what went on-- where I have been. Other people, I don't know. Perhaps-- I have heard, later, that there were other people-- non-Jews arrived in Germany. But quite a number of them died in Germ-- they found out about them. I know quite a number-- They already passed to Germany as non-Jews. But in, in Germany, as I said, later-- or maybe I will say later-- the story how that woman saved me. So there were many methods. They had-- Germans had many methods to find out Jews. And they knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess I'm asking, when you went to the office for the first time, to be processed, do you know what happened to the other people who went before you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in prison, then. As I came out from prison, I was a half a year in prison. [INAUDIBLE] Lackiego. The street was Lackiego.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean, were--

speaker: interviewee
text: So before, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, not before. I mean on the same day as you, they were before you in the queue. Because you said you waited for the other people to go before you. When they were going into the office, do you know what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. No, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any noises or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't hear anything, because I was only two days there. So what happened before I came there, I don't know. At the same time, there was only one small office. And we had to go through that office. And these were, as I said-- I don't know, about 800 people we were, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, from time to time, there came other groups. They came. They didn't go. But the Germans rounded them up. I don't know if they took them from the streets, but there were no Jews. They didn't expect-- suspect any Jews to be among these people. And do you remember the name of that Ukrainian person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never knew. I never knew his name. I wasn't interested. He paid us the money, and that's all. Our wages, and that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we go back to the end of the Russian occupation, in the middle of '41 roughly? You were still working in the laboratory, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I arrived. And then, before the Germans came, they fled, because they knew-- the army knew it-- the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was staying at home. I was back with my childhood home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the main effect, or the main impact, of the Germans coming to Lwow? What were the changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans arrived, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my sister was-- and her husband, with their child, older than mine-- he was about five years-- not full five years, their child-- a little boy. They were just visiting me. They came often. And sometimes they brought me some food, and for my child, because they knew that I am alone and my husband was not anymore with me. And we stood at the window. And that was on second floor. And, below, the Germans, as they arrived-- Lackiego was a very wide road. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They marched, the Germans. I  don't know-- a whole division, or how much. They marched there. And my sister said to me-- so she looked down and said, look-- what nice, lovely young men. These Germans-- all blond, singing, with their heavy boots. You could hear up-- you know, the [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how they were walking straight [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] and singing the German songs. And she said, one by one, they're lovely young men. No one suspected anything. No one believed then. We thought, perhaps it will be better than with the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We're going to continue, next tape.
